title: Supraworld

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions are specific and consistent: the game explicitly calls itself a 'First-Person-Puzzle-Metroidvania' with an interconnected world that opens up through newly found upgrades and abilities, plus heavy emphasis on exploration, secret areas, environmental puzzle solving, and progression-gated traversal. The text repeatedly frames combat as a relatively small, sometimes puzzle-heavy component, which supports metroidvania over an action-led archetype. Detective cases and mystery-solving are notable topical layers, but the core gameplay identity is still ability-gated exploration in a handcrafted interconnected world.

overall_note: The rail has several very strong first-person metroidvania comps, especially the Supraland family and Metroid Prime. Its weakness is breadth: only one non-Supra neighbor meaningfully fits, and Journey to the Savage Planet is noticeably looser because it leans more action-adventure and co-op than puzzle-metroidvania.
anchor_summary: Supraworld is a first-person puzzle-metroidvania centered on ability-gated exploration in a dense interconnected world, with heavy environmental puzzle solving, secret hunting, minimal hand-holding, and relatively light combat.
gap_notes: The Supraland series entries are the clearest fits and should anchor the rail.
Metroid Prime Remastered is a strong genre benchmark, but its combat and scanning-driven structure make it less puzzle-led than the anchor.
Journey to the Savage Planet is understandable as a nearby first-person exploration comp, but it is a weaker fit for the anchor's puzzle-first, low-combat identity.
No candidate meaningfully reflects the anchor's detective-case layer, though that appears secondary to the main exploration-puzzle loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Nearly identical gameplay identity: first-person ability-gated exploration, puzzle-heavy progression, dense secrets, and even less combat than the anchor.
Core template match for the anchor: first-person puzzle-metroidvania, versatile upgrades, interconnected world design, secret hunting, and minimal hand-holding.
Same series formula and priorities—exploration, puzzles, traversal abilities, and reduced combat—though it is a smaller campaign rather than a full-scale entry.
Excellent first-person metroidvania benchmark with interconnected progression and ability gating, but it is more combat-led and less sandbox-puzzly than the anchor.
Shares first-person exploration, tool unlocks, revisiting spaces, and secret finding, but it skews more action-adventure/co-op and less puzzle-metroidvania.

title: Marathon

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and explicitly identify Marathon as a 'PvPvE survival extraction FPS' and a 'PvP extraction shooter.' The core loop is clearly stated: infiltrate/scavenge zones, fight rival players and hostile AI, loot valuables, exfil, then use stolen loot and season-long unlocks to improve future runs. Supporting details include solo or crew-based runs, in-progress lobby insertion, finite supplies, ranked play, moddable weapons, build crafting, vault space expansion, and persistent seasonal progression. This strongly matches extraction_shooter over generic FPS labels or arena/hero shooter near-misses.

overall_note: This is a strong candidate rail because every title meaningfully overlaps with Marathon's extraction-shooter core loop. The best fits match the raid structure, PvPvE pressure, loot-risk economy, and squad-or-solo play; lower entries still fit but diverge more on pace, perspective emphasis, or broader mode identity.
anchor_summary: Marathon is a first-person PvPvE extraction shooter built around infil/scavenge/exfil runs, loot risk, hostile AI plus rival players, solo-or-squad sessions, and persistent progression through builds, factions, and seasonal unlocks.
gap_notes: The set is well centered on extraction shooters rather than generic FPS comps.
Top candidates skew more hardcore/tactical than Marathon's likely more accessible Bungie gunplay and build-forward presentation.
No obvious wrong picks are present; the weakest fit is still understandable because it shares the loot-and-extract session loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The benchmark extraction FPS: first-person raids, PvPvE threats, loadout prep, loot loss on death, exfil-driven progression, and heavy tension per run. More milsim/hardcore than Marathon, but genre-essential.

Very close loop match with solo/duo/trio PvPvE runs, contested objectives, hostile AI, rival squads, and extraction under pressure. Weapon handling and pacing differ, but the session structure is highly aligned.
A particularly apt sci-fi comp: scavenging runs, faction contracts, hostile creatures plus players, evac extraction, and gear/progression upgrades map closely to Marathon's structure.

Strong extraction overlap with tactical looting, PvEvP firefights, squad deployment, contracts, and persistent progress. It leans more open-world and realism-first than Marathon's likely faster, cleaner FPS feel.
Clearly shares the loot-shoot-extract session loop and persistent stash/shelter progression, but it is less of a direct first-person PvPvE extraction benchmark and broader in mode identity.

title: ARC Raiders

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently and concretely describe ARC Raiders as a third-person multiplayer extraction game: players enter distinct topside maps, scavenge loot, survive hostile machines and rival Raiders, then return to the underground hub/workshop to sell loot, craft, repair, upgrade stations, learn blueprints, level skills, and take trader quests. The text explicitly supports solo or up-to-3-player play, PvPvE pressure, repeated incursions with changing map conditions, and progression through gear, crafting, quests, and a branching skill tree. That combination strongly supports extraction_shooter as the core gameplay identity, with loot_shooter and tactical_shooter as secondary affinities rather than primary genre fits.

overall_note: The rail is anchored in the right gameplay neighborhood: extraction shooters with persistent between-run progression, PvPvE pressure, and high-stakes loot retention/loss. Best fits are games that emphasize raid-extract loops plus hub/stash progression; weaker fits drift because of first-person hardcore sim emphasis, boss-bounty structure, or mode profile differences.
anchor_summary: ARC Raiders is a third-person PvPvE extraction shooter built around repeated raids into hostile maps, scavenging loot, fighting AI machines and rival players, then returning to an underground hub to craft, repair, upgrade, quest, and level skills; it supports solo or up-to-3-player play and leans more accessible/tactical than hardcore mil-sim extraction games.
gap_notes: There is no true 'same' candidate here: none match ARC Raiders' exact third-person, squad-friendly, sci-fi PvPvE extraction identity with workshop crafting and trader progression.
The set leans heavily toward first-person or harsher extraction variants, so it somewhat underrepresents ARC Raiders' more approachable co-op third-person feel.
Vigor and Hunt are understandable comps, but each is structurally looser: Vigor is more PvP-forward, while Hunt is more boss-bounty driven than broad scavenging/progression driven.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on the core raid-extract loop: PvPvE drops, hostile wildlife plus players, contracts, loot retention, and gear progression between runs. First-person perspective and a more straightforward prospecting structure keep it below a true same-fit match.

Strong fit through third-person sci-fi PvPvE extraction, underground-to-surface structure, resource scavenging, and persistent progression. It is a bit less aligned because mech/vehicle-centered play and AI-partner systems change the moment-to-moment combat identity.

A foundational extraction comp with raids, stash management, traders, quests, and severe loot-loss stakes. It sits lower because its first-person hardcore mil-sim/RPG structure is much more punishing and simulation-heavy than ARC Raiders.

Third-person extraction structure, loot runs, extraction tension, and shelter upgrading all map well. It is looser because it is more PvP-centric, less defined by PvPvE enemy ecology, and less built around squad-based sci-fi tactical encounters.

Clearly adjacent as a PvPvE extraction shooter with squad tension, AI threats, and sound/stealth management. It is less similar in objective structure and feel: boss-bounty hunts, old-weapon gunplay, and permadeath-style stakes differ meaningfully from ARC Raiders' broader scavenging-and-progression loop.

title: Call of Duty: Black Ops 7

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a classic Call of Duty package centered on first-person gunplay: a co-op/solo campaign with covert military missions, competitive multiplayer with 6v6 and 20v20 maps, and zombies as an additional mode. The copy emphasizes near-future weaponry, mastering an arsenal, Omnimovement, multiplayer map counts, and a signature campaign/multiplayer structure, which strongly supports military_fps over horror-led archetypes. Zombies and psychological-war themes are present, but they read as one component of a broader shooter package rather than the core genre identity of the whole title.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's gameplay identity. It correctly prioritizes other Black Ops entries that share the campaign/multiplayer/Zombies bundle, with Modern Warfare III as the closest non-Black Ops franchise neighbor.
anchor_summary: Call of Duty: Black Ops 7 is best understood as a full-package military FPS: first-person gunplay across a cinematic/co-op campaign, competitive multiplayer, and round-based Zombies, with Black Ops-style near-future movement and progression.
gap_notes: Black Ops 6 is the clearest essential comparison and should anchor the rail.
The strongest fits here are driven by the shared three-pillar Call of Duty structure rather than just franchise branding.
Modern Warfare III fits as a looser Call of Duty package comp, but it is less exact because its co-op/Zombies structure differs from Black Ops 7's round-based emphasis.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Immediate predecessor with the same campaign/multiplayer/round-based Zombies package and very similar Black Ops gunplay, pacing, and player expectations.

title: Making*Lovers: First Blush

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored Steam description explicitly says 'Read a Romantic Comedy' and 'Standard Visual Novel Mechanics,' describing text reading, skipping previously read text, and hiding the text window to view artwork. The content is centered on an authored romance story with four heroines, no combat, and single-player progression through narrative scenes, which strongly matches the visual_novel archetype.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's gameplay identity as a romance-focused Japanese-style visual novel. The base game is an essential inclusion, and the rest are all credible neighboring multi-route romance VNs with similar reading-first structure and heroine-route progression.
anchor_summary: Making*Lovers: First Blush is a single-player romance visual novel centered on reading a lighthearted, multi-heroine romantic comedy with standard VN presentation, route-based progression, and no action-oriented systems.
gap_notes: Making*Lovers should be treated as the obvious must-include benchmark because First Blush appears to be a direct companion/prequel/spinoff within the same gameplay lane.
The remaining candidates are all good fits, but they are broader genre neighbors rather than as exact a match as the base Making*Lovers title.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct Making*Lovers counterpart and the clearest comparison point: same romance-VN format, heroine routes, comedic tone, and dating-focused progression.
Very close fit as a contemporary romantic-comedy visual novel with heroine selection, interactive route progression, and a pure-love focus.
Another light multi-heroine romance VN with the same reading-driven structure and character-route emphasis; slightly less direct than Fureraba but still highly aligned.
Still a solid romance VN comp with route-based heroine stories and authored progression, though its elite-academy setup makes it a touch less everyday-dating aligned than the others.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence is insufficient for taxonomy classification. The title suggests DLC for PowerWash Simulator 2, but no usable gameplay description, mechanics summary, perspective, session structure, or parent-game evidence is provided in the stored DB. The only stored genre/theme signal is a broad 'simulation' label from OpenCritic, which is too generic to map confidently to any allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype.

overall_note: The rail is tightly focused on the PowerWash Simulator family, which is directionally correct. The main gap is that only one candidate is a true parent/base-game match for this DLC; the rest are mostly neighboring franchise entries or DLCs for the prior game rather than equally direct pairings for PowerWash Simulator 2 content.
anchor_summary: Anchor is a licensed DLC for PowerWash Simulator 2, so the best comps are the PowerWash Simulator 2 base game first, then other PowerWash entries and themed DLCs that preserve the same first-person pressure-washing, grime-clearing job loop and relaxed progression structure.

title: Solid Void - Topsy Turvy World

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: The stored evidence clearly describes a standalone single-player puzzle game built around dual-layer nonogram and jigsaw challenges across 250+ puzzles, with calming presentation and difficulty ramping. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a general nonogram/jigsaw puzzle category, and the game does not materially fit hidden_object_puzzle, word_puzzle_strategy, or any other listed archetype. Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

overall_note: The rail has several solid nonogram and art-jigsaw neighbors, but it misses a true same-identity match for the anchor’s defining hybrid of nonogram deduction plus jigsaw assembly. Best candidates capture one half strongly; none fully capture both halves.

title: Minos

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently define Minos as a maze-building roguelite centered on designing and redesigning labyrinth layouts, placing walls/gates/corridors, linking traps through pressure-plate chain reactions, and defending against invading adventurers across repeatable runs. The clearest gameplay identity is systemic construction and trap engineering rather than direct action combat, which aligns best with engineering_sandbox_sim. Roguelite run structure, procedural variation ('Every run brings new enemies, new traps'), and single-player premium replayability are all directly supported. Some presentation details like camera/perspective are not stated, so those fields are left blank.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's reverse-dungeon/trap-defense identity reasonably well, especially with games about constructing killboxes and stopping intruders. The main gap is that several candidates drift toward broader dungeon management RTS or direct action tower defense rather than Minos's specific maze-building roguelite loop.
anchor_summary: Minos is a single-player maze-building roguelite focused on engineering labyrinth layouts, placing and chaining traps, and defending against invading adventurers across repeatable runs. Its identity leans more toward systemic trap construction and reverse-dungeon defense than direct action combat.
gap_notes: No candidate is a near-identical benchmark for Minos's specific combination of maze construction, trap chaining, and roguelite runs.
The set leans partly toward adjacent dungeon-defense and trap-action games, with less emphasis on editable labyrinth design.
Some candidates over-index on direct combat or macro-management compared with Minos's engineering-first structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on building lethal maze-like spaces with modular traps and routing intruders, though its PvP raiding/FPS play and asynchronous multiplayer focus make it less roguelite and less purely defensive than Minos.
Strong reverse-dungeon-defender match with roguelite structure, trap prep, and stopping hero incursions, but it is more management-heavy and turn-based than Minos's maze-engineering sandbox.

Shares evil-dungeon construction, trap placement, and defending against heroes, but its RTS/god-game economy, minion management, and broader strategy scope pull it away from Minos's run-based maze-building identity.

A sensible trap-defense comp because of chained hazards and wave stopping, but it is primarily direct action/tower defense with fixed lanes and active combat rather than player-authored labyrinth building.

Its identity leans more toward systemic trap construction and reverse-dungeon defense than direct action combat.
payload.overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's reverse-dungeon/trap-defense identity reasonably well, especially with games about constructing killboxes and stopping intruders. The main gap is that several candidates drift toward broader dungeon management RTS or direct action tower defense rather than Minos's specific maze-building roguelite loop.
gap_notes: No candidate is a near-identical benchmark for Minos's specific combination of maze construction, trap chaining, and roguelite runs.
The set leans partly toward adjacent dungeon-defense and trap-action games, with less emphasis on editable labyrinth design.
Some candidates over-index on direct combat or macro-management compared with Minos's engineering-first structure.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on building lethal maze-like spaces with modular traps and routing intruders, though its PvP raiding/FPS play and asynchronous multiplayer focus make it less roguelite and less purely defensive than Minos.
Strong reverse-dungeon-defender match with roguelite structure, trap prep, and stopping hero incursions, but it is more management-heavy and turn-based than Minos's maze-engineering sandbox.

Shares evil-dungeon construction, trap placement, and defending against heroes, but its RTS/god-game economy, minion management, and broader strategy scope pull it away from Minos's run-based maze-building identity.

A sensible trap-defense comp because of chained hazards and wave stopping, but it is primarily direct action/tower defense with fixed lanes and active combat rather than player-authored labyrinth building.

title: Town of Zoz

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator copy consistently describe a third-person action-RPG structure: exploring the area around Zoz, helping townspeople through quests, real-time slash-and-dodge combat with a machete, companions with unique combat skills, cooking for buffs, bond-building with residents, and magical weapon/tool enhancement. The strongest fit is a fantasy action RPG with quest exploration and social/progression layers. 'Open world' is not stated outright, so confidence is moderate rather than maximal, but the overall gameplay identity aligns best with open_world_fantasy_action_rpg over alternatives like loot_action_rpg or monster_hunter_style_action_rpg, which lack strong loot-chase or boss-hunt evidence here.

overall_note: The rail has a solid center around cozy town-based action RPGs with gathering/cooking and relationship systems, but several candidates lean much more heavily into life-sim or roguelite restaurant management than the anchor. Best fits are games that combine light action combat with resident bonding and town progression.
anchor_summary: Town of Zoz appears to be a single-player fantasy action RPG with real-time slash-and-dodge combat, companion support, ingredient gathering, cooking for buffs and relationship progression, and a strong town-helping/social quest layer centered on rebuilding bonds with residents.
gap_notes: The set over-indexes on farming/life-sim hybrids; Town of Zoz seems more quest-and-combat driven with cooking/social systems as support layers rather than full daily-life simulation.
Cuisineer shares the food/restaurant hook very strongly, but its roguelite dungeon-crawl structure is a notable structural mismatch versus the anchor's town-quest RPG flow.
Potion Permit matches the community-helping and ingredient-gathering loop well, but its treatment/alchemy focus is less combat-forward and less party-based than the anchor.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very strong overlap in action-RPG adventuring, helping townsfolk, mystery-driven fantasy story, cooking/crafting support systems, and ally-based progression, though Harvestella is more farming/life-sim structured and broader in scope.

Good match on small-town helping loop, gathering materials in the field, relationship-building with residents, and light action gameplay, but its chemist/diagnosis sim focus is less combat-centric and less party-driven than Town of Zoz.

Shares real-time combat, companions, town relationships, cooking, and community tasks, but Rune Factory 5 is much more dominated by farming, routine life-sim systems, and broader sandbox daily structure.

Comparable blend of combat, dungeon exploration, cooking, town bonds, and helping residents, though it is even more of a farming/social-life RPG than the anchor and less centered on the specific hunt-cook-bond loop.

The family restaurant, ingredient gathering, and monster-fighting overlap is obvious, but its procedural roguelite dungeon crawler plus restaurant-management structure is a looser fit than the anchor's narrative town-action RPG identity.

title: Missing The Point

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a short, low-stress single-player fishing-focused experience with handcrafted art, river drifting, landmark observation, and decisions that influence the outcome. However, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not include a fitting fishing-sim or contemplative narrative-sim bucket, and the evidence does not strongly support mapping it to an existing archetype such as life_sim or visual_novel without overreaching. The title appears to center on relaxed fishing and light branching narrative rather than the defining gameplay identities represented in the taxonomy.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's fishing-led, relaxed, narrative-adjacent space reasonably well, with the best fits being other short cozy fishing games. The biggest gap is that several candidates lean much more into RPG progression, building, management, or exploration than the anchor's especially minimal contemplative structure.
anchor_summary: Short single-player low-stress fishing experience centered on drifting down a river, observing landmarks, and making light narrative choices that shape a contemplative story outcome. Its identity is more cozy/reflective fishing-narrative than progression-heavy RPG, management, or challenge-driven fishing game.
gap_notes: Best matches are short cozy fishing games with low stress, but none appear as purely contemplative and choice-outcome driven as the anchor.
Several candidates add heavier progression loops such as gear upgrades, town/shop management, or building systems that materially change the gameplay identity.
The rail trends broader toward cozy exploration and fishing-adjacent games, which is understandable but slightly dilutes the anchor's very focused river-drifting narrative feel.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on short, cozy, low-pressure fishing as the main activity, with a gentle pace and light narrative framing. The garden-building sandbox makes it broader and less purely contemplative than the anchor.
Fishing remains central and it shares a relaxed, story-led structure with character interactions. It is notably more quest-, relationship-, and upgrade-driven, so it is less minimal than the anchor.

A clear fishing-led narrative game with emotional storytelling and relaxed play, but cooking, shopkeeping, town restoration, upgrades, and optional co-op make it a fuller slice-of-life RPG loop than the anchor.

Strong fishing-and-discovery overlap plus narrative mystery, but the boat upgrades, economy, navigation risk, and tension-heavy survival pressure create a substantially different challenge model and tone of play.

It matches the anchor's short, peaceful, contemplative vibe and light character interactions, but fishing is only a side activity; the core loop is exploration and traversal rather than fishing-centered progression or story outcomes.

title: Prop Sumo

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes Prop Sumo as a 'party fighting game' centered on PvP matches where players brawl with melee moves, chain light and heavy attacks, use items, and compete for score across arena maps. The Steam detailed description adds strong fighting-game signals: 1v1 and free-for-all match formats, a Training Dojo to master moves and combos, unlockable ultimates, and round-based progression. This supports a primary identity of traditional_fighter, with party_game as a secondary flavor due to chaotic multiplayer arenas, items, and friend-focused free-for-all play.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor's party-combat identity fairly well, especially with physics-brawler and multiplayer arena-fighter comps. Best fits are games centered on chaotic versus sessions, stage hazards, item interactions, and casual-competitive free-for-alls. The weaker points are several 2D platform fighters, which match versus structure and itemized combat but diverge in perspective, movement model, and precision/combo expectations from Prop Sumo's 3D party-brawler feel.
anchor_summary: Prop Sumo is a multiplayer party-brawler: short PvP arena matches for 2-8 players, melee-heavy attacks and charge moves, items and hazards, score/lives rulesets, 1v1 or free-for-all structure, and light progression/unlocks around repeated versus sessions.
gap_notes: The set leans a bit too heavily on platform fighters relative to 3D physics/arena brawlers.
Party-first melee sandbox comps are strongest here; those should outrank more formal fighting benchmarks.
No obvious must-keep/base-game equivalent is present, but Gang Beasts and Party Animals are the clearest editorial fits.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on chaotic 3D melee party brawling, hazardous arenas, knockout-focused rounds, and local/online PvP. Less combo-structured than Prop Sumo, but the session feel is highly aligned.

Excellent fit for party-first arena scuffles with physics, items, environmental interaction, and casual-competitive multiplayer. Slightly broader minigame/physics-party identity than Prop Sumo's more fighter-like brawling.

Short-round multiplayer combat, power-ups, hazards, simple controls, and party pacing line up well. Main gap is its boomerang-centric top-down/ranged design rather than Prop Sumo's 3D melee brawler structure.

Strong benchmark for free-for-all fighting with items, stage hazards, custom rules, and versus mastery. However, it is a precise 2D platform fighter with ring-out design, noticeably different from Prop Sumo's 3D party-brawl feel.
Matches on training, versus depth, item/gadget spawns, and support for 1v1 and larger casual modes. Still a 2D platform fighter first, so the movement/combat model is a step away from the anchor.

title: Beyond Words

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Beyond Words as a word-based strategic puzzle roguelite: 'fusion of roguelike strategy and word-crafting,' with runs shaped by letter placement, modifiers, stickers, booster/power cards, upgrades, synergies, seeded runs, time attacks, and boss challenges. The gameplay loop is tactical board optimization and build construction across repeated runs, with no combat signals. Strong fit for word_puzzle_strategy rather than action, simulation, or hidden-object categories.

overall_note: The rail has one very strong structural comp and two reasonable word-game neighbors, but none fully match the anchor's specific mix of word-building plus roguelite board-planning puzzle play. Good starting set, though still missing a truer same-identity benchmark if one exists in catalog.
anchor_summary: Beyond Words is a single-player word puzzle strategy roguelite built around letter placement, board optimization, combo/synergy building, upgrades and modifiers, boss challenges, and repeatable run-based mastery modes like seeded runs and time attack. Its identity is puzzle-first and tactical rather than combat-first.
gap_notes: No candidate is a true 'same' match for a word-first tactical roguelite board puzzle with run-based buildcraft.
The set leans either toward word-combat RPG hybrids or toward non-word roguelite score-optimization, leaving the anchor's exact board-planning identity underrepresented.
Balatro is structurally very relevant for run design and synergy stacking, but it does not cover the anchor's wordplay loop.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Excellent match on run-based progression, score chasing, modifier stacking, boss/challenge structure, seeded runs, and synergy-heavy optimization; weaker on the actual verb since it is a card/poker deckbuilder rather than a word puzzle.

Strong word-construction overlap plus upgrades and endless/challenge play, but the core loop is turn-based word combat/RPG encounters rather than tactical board planning across roguelite-style puzzle runs.

Shares word-making, upgrades, items, bosses, and compact repeatable progression, but its action-RPG combat framing and monster-slaying focus move it further from Beyond Words' puzzle-first strategic board optimization.

title: Incantation

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Incantation as a first-person horror mystery game centered on exploring an eerie cult village, searching for clues, hiding from danger, and uncovering the truth behind a missing daughter. The repeated emphasis on fear, madness, mystery-solving, and stealth-based survival supports psychological_horror as the best fit, with stealth_action_adventure as a secondary influence. Evidence for combat-driven survival-horror is weak, so the classification stays with horror exploration and stealth rather than action combat.

overall_note: The set is directionally solid for first-person narrative horror with exploration and evasion. Best fits emphasize vulnerable movement, mystery-solving, and stealth over combat. The main gap is that several picks lean more atmospheric or survival-horror than Incantation’s specific first-person horror-mystery plus stealth profile.
anchor_summary: Incantation appears to be a first-person horror mystery centered on vulnerable exploration, clue-finding, stealthy avoidance of danger, and story-driven uncovering of a cult-linked family disappearance, with little evidence of combat-forward survival systems.
gap_notes: No direct base-game/DLC style benchmark exists here, so the rail relies on neighbors rather than an essential must-include counterpart.
The strongest matches are first-person, story-led horror with evasion and investigation; candidates that are more purely atmospheric or more specialized survival-horror are somewhat looser fits.
There is mild over-weighting toward shared occult/family horror themes; gameplay fit is strongest where stealth and vulnerable exploration are explicit.

The evidence is strong for gameplay identity, but it does not strongly support any allowed primary archetype without forcing a misleading fit such as action-adventure or hidden-object puzzle.

overall_note: The set is directionally strong for non-combat, emotionally driven puzzle-adventures, with the top three fitting the anchor especially well. The weaker end drifts toward more linear puzzle-platforming and less exploratory structure, but none are outright off-base.

title: Pokemon Champions

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence clearly describes a multiplayer-focused Pokémon battle product centered on traditional Pokémon battle mechanics, with Ranked, Casual, and Private Battles plus Pokémon HOME compatibility. However, the available taxonomy archetypes do not include a strong fit for a match-based turn-based monster battling game without the broader campaign, exploration, or creature-collection loop expected by monster_collect_rpg. Accepting it under an existing archetype would overstate unsupported elements such as adventure structure or collection gameplay.

overall_note: The rail has a strong lead comp in Pokémon Stadium, but the rest are mostly broader mainline monster-collect RPGs that share battle mechanics and competitive team-building more than they share Champions' match-based, battle-only structure.
anchor_summary: Pokémon Champions is a standalone, multiplayer-focused Pokémon battle platform built around traditional turn-based Pokémon combat, team-building, and online ranked/casual/private matches, without the broader exploration-and-capture campaign loop of mainline entries.
gap_notes: Pokémon Stadium is the clearest structural match because it is also a battle-centric Pokémon spinoff rather than a full adventure RPG.
The mainline entries share core combat rules and roster-building logic, but their primary identity is still campaign progression, exploration, and collection.
Modern competitive relevance helps Sword and Shield somewhat, but they remain adjacent rather than same-identity comps.
Scarlet & Violet and Sun/Moon are understandable neighbors, though both are notably looser fits than a dedicated battle-platform comp.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Battle-focused Pokémon spinoff built around traditional turn-based matches, multiplayer, cups, and imported teams; the closest structural and audience match to a standalone Pokémon battle hub.
Shares modern Pokémon battle systems, team-building, and competitive play conventions, but its core loop is still a full campaign RPG with catching, exploration, and story progression.
Uses the same underlying Pokémon battle logic and trainer progression framework, but it is primarily an adventure RPG centered on catching, travel, and campaign advancement.
Current-generation Pokémon combat overlaps well, but the open-world exploration, freeform progression, and collection-heavy campaign make it farther from Champions’ battle-platform identity.

title: The Occultist

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored official descriptions consistently define The Occultist as a first-person narrative thriller with explicit survival-horror framing: 'Survival horror: The Occultist has all the distinctive elements of the genre,' combined with tense exploration, supernatural threats, ominous cult imagery, and a horror-focused abandoned-island setting. The gameplay loop centers on exploring Godstone, investigating the protagonist's father's disappearance and occult rituals, using a mystic pendulum to interact with and modify the environment, and crucially avoiding direct combat: 'Alan always avoids direct combat' and progresses via 'Hide and Sneak' stealth mechanics. That strongly supports survival_horror as the primary identity, with psychological_horror and stealth_action_adventure as meaningful secondary overlaps.

overall_note: The candidate set is broadly on-target for first-person survival horror, with several strong stealth-and-vulnerability comps. Best fits are games built around fleeing, hiding, and investigative progression; the weaker fits lean more toward psychological walking-horror or more systemic action-survival than the anchor appears to.
anchor_summary: The Occultist is a first-person single-player survival horror game centered on tense exploration, supernatural investigation, environmental interaction via a pendulum, and stealthy avoidance of threats rather than direct combat. The closest comps should emphasize first-person vulnerability, narrative progression, exploration/puzzle solving, and hide-and-sneak survival over action-heavy combat.
gap_notes: A top rail for this anchor should prioritize first-person survival horror titles with explicit no-combat or minimal-combat stealth avoidance.

Most candidates fit the broad genre, but not all match the anchor's likely balance of narrative thriller pacing and stealth-forward progression.

title: Samson

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Samson as a single-player open-world urban action game, not a pure racer. The core loop centers on moving through open world city districts, taking consequential jobs/missions under a debt-and-time-pressure structure, engaging in close-quarters brawler combat, and using cars as traversal/combat tools during chases and collisions. Repeated signals include 'Open World Districts,' 'Brawler Combat,' 'Cars as Weapons,' climbing through alleys and rooftops, a 25+ upgrade skill tree, law-response escalation, and story-driven progress tied to Samson's sister and criminal debt. Racing is present, but the evidence frames driving as one major verb inside a broader open-world action-adventure campaign rather than the sole governing loop.

overall_note: The rail has several credible urban open-world crime/action neighbors, with Sleeping Dogs and Mad Max standing out most for the melee-plus-driving identity. The main weakness is candidate quality/control: one entry is a Mafia III DLC rather than the base game, and Saints Row here refers to the 2022 reboot, which is a looser fit than the requested Saints Row 2 concept. Overall direction is solid, but the set is somewhat polluted by edition/version mismatches and a few candidates that lean more shooter-sandbox than brawler-driven action.
anchor_summary: Samson appears to be a single-player third-person open-world urban action game built around close-quarters brawler combat, weaponized driving, mission/job choices under time and debt pressure, police/law escalation, traversal through city districts, and character upgrades. Best comps should preserve the mix of street-level open-world crime structure, on-foot melee emphasis, and cars as an active combat/chase tool rather than focusing mainly on shooting, pure driving, or tonal overlap.
gap_notes: Sleeping Dogs is the clearest benchmark here and should anchor the rail.
Mad Max is an unusually strong non-urban neighbor because it shares the melee-plus-vehicular-combat core loop and upgrade-driven open-world structure.
Mafia III: Stones Unturned is a DLC entry, not Mafia III base game, so it is materially weaker for a similar-games rail despite some overlap.
Grand Theft Auto IV fits the grounded urban crime sandbox angle, but its combat identity is more gun/sandbox-driven than brawler-centric.
The provided Saints Row candidate is the 2022 reboot rather than Saints Row 2, making it a looser and more chaotic sandbox comparison than the requested rationale suggests.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Open-world city crime campaign with strong hand-to-hand combat, driving woven into missions, police pressure, and a grounded street-level structure that closely matches Samson’s melee-and-driving identity.

Very close on third-person open-world structure, brutal melee, vehicle combat/chases, and upgrade progression; less urban and more survival/scavenging than Samson, but the gameplay mix is highly aligned.

Grounded open-world city crime game with driving, wanted-level escalation, and mission-based progression, but its combat identity leans more toward shooting and sandbox play than close-quarters brawler focus.

Open-city crime sandbox with driving and faction conflict, but this specific Saints Row is the 2022 reboot and skews more exaggerated, shooter-driven, and chaos-sandbox oriented than Samson’s tighter brutal brawler framing.
The overlap to Mafia III exists in theory, but this candidate is Stones Unturned DLC rather than the base game, so it is not a clean or reliable similar-game recommendation for Samson.

title: Starfield - Terran Armada

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted as western_narrative_rpg using DLC inheritance guidance. Terran Armada is explicitly described as a story DLC for Starfield, adding new locations, technologies, weapons, and enemy encounters, with no indication of a mode or genre shift. The parent game's stored descriptions strongly establish the core identity: Bethesda-style single-player sci-fi RPG with character creation, faction and side quests, dialogue-driven roleplay, open-ended planetary exploration, ship piloting/combat, loot/crafting, and outpost building across a long-form campaign. That evidence supports a quest-driven open-world western narrative RPG more strongly than a pure shooter or action-adventure classification. The DLC copy is additive and fully consistent with that parent gameplay identity.

overall_note: The proposed rail is very strong. It correctly centers the parent game, then clusters close Bethesda-style open-world RPGs and nearby single-player sci-fi action RPGs. The top of the list is especially solid for gameplay identity rather than just setting.
anchor_summary: Starfield - Terran Armada is a story DLC that inherits Starfield's core identity: a single-player Bethesda-style open-world sci-fi RPG with first/third-person combat, quest-driven exploration, character builds, loot/crafting, and long-form sandbox progression.
gap_notes: Starfield is an essential base-game inclusion and should remain the clear #1.
Fallout 4 and Skyrim are especially strong because they match Bethesda's open-world quest sandbox structure more closely than most other sci-fi games.
The Outer Worlds is a strong fit on first-person sci-fi RPG fundamentals, though it is more hub-based and compact than Starfield.
Mass Effect: Andromeda is relevant but looser due to its more directed squad-centric action RPG structure and multiplayer component.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Direct parent game of the DLC with the same core systems, progression, combat, exploration structure, and audience expectation.
Very close Bethesda sandbox RPG comp: first-person exploration, shooting, looting, questing, crafting, and player-driven build progression.

title: ChainStaff

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently identifies ChainStaff as a 'brutal action-platformer' and '2D Platformer' with a transforming spear/grappling weapon, swinging and traversal, mutating hordes, and 'bone-shaking boss fights.' The core loop is side-scrolling combat plus platforming through a handcrafted campaign. The text also supports a single-player premium campaign structure, hand-drawn surreal presentation, grotesque sci-fi/horror setting, and some authored branching via rescue-or-harvest choices leading to different endings. Evidence is strong for action_platformer and does not strongly support a different primary archetype such as metroidvania or character_action.

overall_note: The set captures ChainStaff's combat-first 2D action-platformer identity fairly well, especially candidates with grapple-enabled traversal and stage-driven boss pacing. The main weakness is that some picks lean too far into metroidvania exploration or combo-character-action compared with ChainStaff's more focused action-platformer structure.

title: Eko and the Bewitched Lands

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify a single-player action RPG with randomized worlds, roguelike run structure, fast dodge-heavy combat, bosses, weapons/abilities/artifacts, per-run upgrades, and permanent meta progression. That strongly supports an action-RPG identity with replayable build variation. The main ambiguity is taxonomy fit: the game reads like a single-player action roguelite, but that exact archetype is not available in the allowed list, so loot_action_rpg is the closest supported primary archetype, with character_action as secondary due to fast aggressive dodge-based combat.

overall_note: The set is directionally correct for a fast action-roguelite rail. The best fits are other top-down, run-based combat games with dodge timing, build variation, and meta progression. The main gap is that none of the candidates fully capture Eko's lighter action-adventure/puzzle angle, and several candidates lean more into co-op or hero-based structures than the anchor appears to.
anchor_summary: Top-down or overhead-feeling single-player action roguelite/action RPG: fast dodge-heavy real-time combat, randomized runs/worlds, per-run build upgrades, weapons/abilities/artifacts, bosses, and permanent meta progression, with some light puzzle/exploration flavor.
gap_notes: No candidate clearly mirrors the anchor's action-adventure plus puzzle/exploration component; most comps are more combat-room-forward.

The strongest matches are roguelite action games rather than a pure loot_action_rpg reading, suggesting the anchor's closest practical neighborhood is action roguelite/top-down hack-and-slash.

title: Machine Child

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Machine Child as a non-combat girl-raising/nurturing simulation. The gameplay loop centers on guiding a daughter through life in a town via 700+ story events, choosing activities, outfits, and developmental paths, building affection, and reaching 22 different endings. That strongly fits life_sim with branching, relationship-driven progression and replayable single-player outcomes. Visual-novel adjacency is present through authored event scenes and multiple endings, but the core identity is life simulation rather than pure VN.

overall_note: The rail is well targeted. It includes the core daughter-raising benchmarks first, then broader stat/choice-driven raising sims. The biggest strength is that the top candidates share Machine Child's central identity of shaping a girl's future through repeated developmental choices and branching endings.
anchor_summary: Machine Child is a single-player daughter-raising life sim: guide a girl through authored events, choose activities and outfits, build affection, shape stats/pathing, and reach many branching endings. Best matches should share the parenting/raising loop, schedule or choice-driven development, and replayable outcome-focused progression rather than just general narrative or fantasy vibes.
gap_notes: Princess Maker-style games are the clearest benchmark space for this anchor, and the proposed set correctly centers that lineage.
Volcano Princess and Ciel Fledge fit especially well because they preserve the explicit daughter-raising structure while modernizing presentation and systems.
Long Live The Queen is a valid looser comp due to weekly planning, stat checks, and branching outcomes, but it is more survival/queen-training than nurturing daughter-raising.

title: Sigma Star Saga DX

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Sigma Star Saga DX as a deliberate hybrid: top-down planetary RPG-style exploration combined with side-scrolling shoot-'em-up space battles, with branching story, multiple endings, boss battles, tool-based exploration, and weapon customization via Gun Data. While the evidence is strong for the game's mixed identity, the available Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetypes do not cleanly cover this exploration-plus-shmup structure. Candidate RPG fits in the computed taxonomy are incomplete and rely on weak or mismatched assumptions about combat structure and perspective. Because its core gameplay loop materially spans an unsupported genre combination rather than aligning strongly to a single allowed archetype, rejection is more appropriate than forcing an inaccurate classification.

overall_note: The rail has one especially strong hybrid comparison and two looser but defensible exploration/action neighbors. The main gap is that most candidates capture only part of the anchor's identity rather than its full exploration-plus-shmup split.
anchor_summary: Sigma Star Saga DX is a rare hybrid built around two equal gameplay layers: top-down action-RPG/adventure exploration with tool-gated progression and branching story, plus separate side-scrolling shmup battles with weapon customization and boss fights. title: KuloNiku: Bowl Up!

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present a restaurant-management/cooking simulation loop: inherit and restore a family restaurant, take customer orders, prepare meals through tactile cooking actions, upgrade tools and ingredients, customize the shop, and earn gameplay benefits from decorations. Progression is driven by improving the restaurant and building friendship levels with town residents, with a light authored story around rivals and family legacy. Although 'Meatball Brawls' are framed competitively, they are still culinary scoring duels rather than combat, so the core identity remains management sim/tycoon rather than action. Evidence is strong enough to accept management_tycoon, with some life-sim adjacency from relationship-building and town social elements.

overall_note: The set is well focused on food-service and restaurant-management sims, with strong overlap on order fulfillment, venue upgrades, customization, and cozy progression. The main gap is that several candidates lean either more management-heavy or more farming/gathering-heavy than the anchor’s tactile cooking-plus-social-restoration blend.
anchor_summary: KuloNiku: Bowl Up! is a cozy single-player restaurant-management sim centered on hands-on food prep, fulfilling customer orders, upgrading tools and ingredients, customizing the restaurant, and progressing through social relationships and culinary rivalry events rather than combat.
gap_notes: No candidate fully matches the anchor's combination of hands-on cooking, restaurant restoration, town relationship progression, and judged cooking-battle structure.

title: People of Note

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic descriptions consistently identify People of Note as a turn-based RPG centered on recruiting a band of party members, traveling across multiple themed regions, and progressing through a story campaign. The combat loop is explicitly party-based and tactical, with timing-based rhythm inputs layered onto turn-based encounters via musical mashup attacks and changing combat conditions. This supports a JRPG-story-RPG identity first, with tactical RPG elements secondary. The rhythm component appears additive to the core party-managed campaign RPG structure rather than defining the game as a standalone rhythm genre.

overall_note: The rail is directionally solid: most candidates are story-led turn-based JRPG neighbors, and several correctly capture the anchor's blend of party progression and active-input combat. The main weakness is that the set leans broad Mario/Persona JRPG rather than consistently prioritizing games where timed or rhythm-like interaction is central to the combat loop.
anchor_summary: People of Note is a single-player, authored JRPG-style campaign built around party recruitment/management, region-to-region exploration, dungeons, and turn-based battles enhanced by rhythm/timed inputs and team synergies. The clearest comps should therefore be story-driven party RPGs with interactive turn-based combat, not just games with music themes or broad JRPG branding.
gap_notes: The strongest fits are games with turn-based party RPG structure plus persistent timed-input or reactive battle mechanics; only part of the rail consistently targets that.
The rail underrepresents music- or rhythm-forward RPG neighbors, which would better reflect the anchor's identity-driving combat presentation.
One candidate is a good JRPG audience overlap pick but is structurally looser because of its calendar/social-sim layer.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on authored JRPG progression, overworld exploration, party-based turn-based combat, and constant timed button presses that make battles actively interactive.

title: Hidden around the World

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Store and aggregator descriptions consistently identify the game as a cozy hidden-object adventure built around exploring hand-drawn city scenes, finding hidden objects, completing relaxing missions, unlocking new scenes, and decorating locations. The gameplay loop is discovery/puzzle-oriented rather than combat- or platform-focused, with single-player progression across handcrafted destinations. Metacritic also explicitly labels the genre as Hidden Object, reinforcing hidden_object_puzzle as the best fit.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's hidden-object identity. The best matches emphasize calm scene-scanning, hand-drawn environments, progression through stages, and in several cases creation/sharing features that strongly mirror the anchor's level-sharing hook. The only looser fit is the more point-and-click adventure-leaning Please, Touch The Artwork 2.
anchor_summary: Cozy single-player hidden-object puzzle/adventure centered on scanning hand-drawn scenes, completing relaxed find missions, unlocking new locations, adding light decoration/customization, and supporting user-created level sharing.
gap_notes: A direct must-keep comparison is present in Hidden in My Paradise, from the same creators and with nearly identical find/decorate/share structure.
The rail skews toward pure hidden-scene search games; that is appropriate for the anchor's core loop.
Only one candidate is meaningfully adjacent rather than tightly matched: Please, Touch The Artwork 2 adds more adventure/puzzle tasking beyond straightforward hidden-object scanning.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Closest match: same developer, same cozy hidden-object loop, hand-crafted scenes, mission-based progression, decoration, and user level creation/sharing.

title: A Rat's Quest: The Way Back Home - Season 1

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored Steam and Metacritic copy consistently describe a single-player, narrative-driven action adventure centered on movement and navigation: 'climb, jump, crawl, slither, and sneak,' with explicit 'hybrid gameplay' switching between side and top-down perspectives. Combat is clearly present but not dominant over traversal, with a sword, throwing daggers, survival against threats, and 'challenging boss encounters.' The game is framed as a cinematic authored journey with voiced cutscenes, supporting a long-form linear campaign. While the cinematic framing is strong, the core playable identity in the evidence is platform-navigation-led action with stealth elements, making action_platformer the best fit.

overall_note: The rail captures the anchor’s small-hero, traversal-forward, danger-in-oversized-spaces identity fairly well, with several strong cinematic stealth/action-adventure neighbors. The main weakness is a couple of candidates that drift toward very different combat/RPG or VR-assisted play structures.
anchor_summary: Single-player cinematic action-adventure/action-platformer with hybrid side/top-down traversal, stealthy movement verbs, light-to-moderate direct combat, boss encounters, and a linear story campaign built around navigating oversized hostile spaces as a small creature.
gap_notes: There is no near-identical hybrid cinematic action-platformer comp here; most candidates match only parts of the anchor.
The best fits emphasize stealthy traversal and vulnerable navigation, but several candidates underrepresent the anchor’s direct combat and boss-fight component.
VR-mediated play and heavy action-RPG progression are meaningful structural mismatches for this anchor.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Excellent fit on tiny-protagonist stealthy navigation, dangerous oversized environments, authored questing, and single-player adventure structure. More immersive sim/RPG and less platformer-combat driven than the anchor, so not quite same.

Very close on linear cinematic progression, hostile oversized spaces, climbing/hiding/squeezing traversal, and tension-based survival. It is more horror-puzzle and chase-driven, with much lighter sustained combat than the anchor.

Strong match for cinematic side-scrolling stealth-platforming, fragile-hero survival, lethal set pieces, and authored progression through dangerous spaces. Older puzzle-stealth emphasis and weaker action-combat layer keep it from being closer.

Shares small-rat side-scrolling action, dangerous creatures, and boss-oriented progression, but its core identity is much more punishing combat/RPG focused and less stealth-traversal-cinematic than the anchor.

There is some overlap in tiny-hero action-adventure, exploration, and combat, but the VR framing and player-assist puzzle structure create a meaningfully different core loop and audience expectation from the anchor’s standard campaign.

title: All Will Fall

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify All Will Fall as a 'survival city builder/colony sim' centered on physics-based 3D construction, resource management, logistics, production chains, societal needs, research progression, random events, and sandbox/scenario play. The core loop is building and optimizing a settlement rather than commanding armies or pursuing direct combat. Overhead strategy presentation, base/colony growth, systemic simulation, and optimization pressure strongly support city_builder as the primary archetype, with colony_sim and factory_builder as secondary overlaps due faction needs, survival management, and supply-chain emphasis.

overall_note: The rail is directionally strong for survival city-builder fans, with good coverage of colony management, pressure, and scenario-driven settlement optimization. Its main gap is that only Timberborn closely reflects the anchor’s standout 3D/vertical construction and systemic building-physics identity; the rest lean more toward broader survival city-building than that specific structural sandbox niche.
anchor_summary: All Will Fall is a single-player survival city builder/colony sim focused on overhead settlement construction, resource logistics, faction/population needs, research, scenario/sandbox play, and a distinctive physics-based vertical building system where structural collapse is a core challenge.
gap_notes: The set underrepresents games with comparable vertical/stacked construction as a primary mechanic.
Only one candidate meaningfully echoes the anchor’s physics-driven building and collapse considerations.
Most candidates fit survival colony management well, but several are looser on sandbox construction freedom and logistics-led city design.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Very close on survival city-building, vertical architecture, systemic resource chains, faction differences, and environmental simulation; especially strong because 3D construction and water/terrain systems mirror the anchor’s structural-planning appeal.

Excellent match for scenario-driven survival city management, population needs, harsh tradeoffs, and difficult policy decisions, though it is less of a freeform construction sandbox and lacks the anchor’s vertical physics-building focus.

Good fit through pressure-heavy city/colony management, infrastructure balancing, exploration, and survival decision-making, but its closed habitat structure and space-station management are less construction-sandbox-driven than the anchor.

Solid post-apocalyptic colony builder with research, expeditions, disasters, and population management, but it is more conventional settlement survival and less defined by vertical building, physics, or dense logistics construction.

title: SHINOBI: Art of Vengeance - SEGA Villains DLC

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Accepted via DLC inheritance from the parent game because the DLC is clearly additive rather than mode-shifting: it adds 5 stages, 3 bosses, ninpos, skins, music, and boss rush modes. The parent game's stored description explicitly defines the core gameplay as 'an all-new 2D action platformer' with precision ninja combat, combos, obstacle-overcoming tools, challenging platforming puzzles, hidden secrets, and multiple stages. That strongly supports action_platformer with side-scrolling 2D stage-based progression, dominant combat, platform traversal, boss encounters, and precision platforming. The DLC's boss-rush/stage content reinforces rather than contradicts that identity.

overall_note: The rail has a correct essential inclusion in the parent game and several strong 2D ninja action-platformer neighbors. The main weakness is one candidate that matches on ninja combat intensity more than on perspective and structure.
anchor_summary: This DLC inherits the parent game's identity: a single-player 2D side-scrolling action-platformer centered on ninja combat, stage-based progression, platforming challenges, boss fights, and additive DLC content like new stages and boss rush.

Cyber Shadow and The Messenger are the closest non-parent fits because they share 2D ninja action-platforming, bosses, and ability progression.

title: Cursed Words: The Word Game That Isn't

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify Cursed Words as a roguelike word/strategy game built around repeated runs, stage-based progression, synergy-building upgrades, and rule-bending modifiers. The Steam detailed description gives concrete loop evidence: each run has five stages with encounters and a boss, victory at Stage 5, challenge tiers afterward, and build-defining systems via Character, Sticker upgrades, Stamp loadout, and consumable Tiles. This strongly supports a strategic word-puzzle identity with roguelite/deck-growth structure rather than an action genre. Combat is metaphorical ('boss encounters') but the gameplay described is non-combat, methodical, and score/synergy focused.

overall_note: The rail has one excellent structural comp and one strong word-mechanics comp, but it is thin and misses more direct roguelike word-run peers. Balatro fits the anchor's run structure and score-synergy identity very well despite using cards instead of words; Letter Quest fits the word-combat layer well but is less aligned on roguelike run structure and build-crafting depth.
anchor_summary: A single-player roguelike word-puzzle strategy game built around repeated runs, stage-and-boss structure, and heavy synergy construction through persistent loadout choices and run-specific modifiers that bend the rules of word formation and scoring.
gap_notes: The set is small and lacks a near-direct roguelike word-game comparator.
There is only one candidate that clearly matches the anchor's run-based synergy-exploitation structure.
Word construction is represented, but long-form roguelite progression and challenge-tier structure are under-covered.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Excellent match on run-based structure, escalating score thresholds, boss/blind checkpoints, and rule-breaking synergy builds. Different verb set than word play, but the gameplay identity is very close.
Strong overlap in forming words to drive encounters and buying upgrades between battles, but it reads more like a turn-based word RPG/endless mode game than a deep roguelike synergy-run builder.

title: ROGOLF

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify the game as a minigolf roguelite: each run is different, levels have their own rules, players spend shots as score, collect coins for multipliers, buy equipment/power-ups, and progress through gates toward higher stages. This is strong evidence for a run-based score-chasing sports/arcade structure with roguelite modifiers. However, the allowed Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a fitting minigolf/golf-arcade/roguelite sports archetype. 'sports_sim' is a poor fit because the game is not presented as simulation-focused, and other archetypes are even less appropriate. Rejected due to taxonomy coverage mismatch rather than lack of gameplay evidence.

overall_note: The set captures the anchor's two main axes reasonably well: golf/minigolf course play and run-based roguelite/upgrade structure. The best candidate combines both directly, while the rest each cover only one side strongly.
anchor_summary: ROGOLF is a run-based arcade minigolf roguelite: shot-limited holes, coin/multiplier scoring, equipment and power-up purchasing, rule-changing stages, and progression through gates toward higher floors.
gap_notes: The rail has only one clearly direct golf-roguelite comparison; most others split between minigolf without roguelite structure or roguelites with non-golf shot mechanics.
Multiplayer/party minigolf candidates are somewhat overrepresented relative to ROGOLF's run-based progression identity.
A stronger rail would prioritize more single-player arcade sports or physics-shot roguelites if available.

candidate_reviews.rationale: The clearest fit: golf-first play, every shot as a scarce resource, hazard-heavy hole progression, power-ups, replayability, and explicit roguelite structure align very closely with ROGOLF.
Very close on arcade minigolf, gimmick courses, score/challenge focus, and power-ups. It lacks the anchor's run-based roguelite economy, but the core on-course play is highly relevant.
Strong match on run-based progression, shops/relics, and physics-based aiming, but its combat-driven pachinko loop is materially different from minigolf hole completion and score-gating.
Shares physics-shot play, randomized runs, loot, and roguelite progression, but it is fundamentally a dungeon crawler with character classes and enemy combat rather than a golf game.

Understandable because it is obstacle-heavy minigolf with power-ups, but its party/multiplayer focus and mode structure are a notable mismatch for ROGOLF's single-run upgrade-driven roguelite identity.

title: Breath of Fire IV

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently present Breath of Fire IV as a classic single-player JRPG adventure with a fixed cast of party members, skill and magic mastery, strategic battles, dragon transformations, and an authored story following Ryu, Nina, and companions across a fantasy world. Steam and Metacritic both support a long-form campaign structure and JRPG identity; the gameplay copy emphasizes party-based strategic combat and story progression, not match-based or fighting-game play. The current computed near-miss for traditional_fighter appears to come from misleading phrases like 'go head to head' and 'combo actions,' but the broader evidence clearly supports a story-driven party RPG.

title: Resident Evil 3: Nemesis

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence strongly supports classic survival horror. Steam and corpus text explicitly state third person perspective, horror/zombie themes, escaping Raccoon City alive, and relying on brute force and cunning against terrifying mutations. Metacritic directly says it is part of the series that made the survival horror genre. The descriptions imply a linear authored campaign with encounter-driven enemy pressure, deliberate survival-oriented combat, and a single-player focus; Mercenaries Mode adds a secondary match-style mode without changing the core identity.

overall_note: The rail has two strong Resident Evil comps and one broader survival-horror neighbor. It is missing an especially obvious classic-era fixed-camera comp set, but the provided candidates are mostly solid.

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a 2D side-scrolling action title centered on platforming and repeated boss battles. The strongest signals are side-scrolling presentation, dominant melee combat, boss-centric structure, and a single-player campaign loop. The copy emphasizes combos, unlockable skills and charms, and preparing builds before battles, which supports action-platformer progression with some metroidvania/beat-em-up adjacency. However, exploration, backtracking, and discovery are not described strongly enough to make metroidvania the primary archetype, and the boss-focused structure is less typical of a pure beat 'em up.

title: Fishbowl

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored descriptions repeatedly and explicitly call Fishbowl a 'visual novel' and a narrative slice-of-life coming-of-age story. The core loop centers on making choices over the course of a month, video-calling friends/family/coworkers, doing daily care/work tasks, and sorting through belongings and memory-related puzzles. The text stresses 'no bad endings, just different paths' and says 'the choices you make will shape the possibilities in Alo's future,' which strongly supports authored branching and dialogue/relationship-focused interaction. No combat is described anywhere; instead the gameplay is framed as intimate, cozy, grief-focused narrative progression with light puzzle/matching activities. That combination fits visual_novel much better than broader adventure labels.

overall_note: The set is directionally strong for narrative-first, low-challenge, emotionally intimate games, with one especially close visual-novel comp. The main gap is that several picks drift toward adventure exploration or highly linear interactive art pieces rather than Fishbowl’s choice-shaped slice-of-life visual novel structure.
anchor_summary: Fishbowl is a single-player narrative visual novel centered on low-challenge daily-life choices, conversations, light memory/puzzle tasks, and emotionally intimate coming-of-age/grief storytelling over a month-long structure.
gap_notes: The rail has a strong top pick, but overall it leans a bit broad around 'emotional narrative' instead of consistently matching visual-novel-style daily-life progression.
Several candidates share tone and themes more than core loop; Fishbowl’s routine of conversations, choices, and light task/puzzle play is more specific than pure narrative art pieces.
The set could use more titles with explicit branching or day-by-day life-structure similar to Fishbowl’s month-long personal progression.

title: Clean Up Earth

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored evidence consistently describes a relaxing environmental restoration/cleanup game focused on vacuuming waste, recycling materials, rebuilding damaged structures, exploring large maps, and cooperating with up to 25 players. The strongest supported traits are multiplayer/co-op support, large shared spaces, casual/simulation framing, and non-combat restorative play. However, the Similar Games Taxonomy V2 archetype list does not contain a suitable home for a cooperative cleanup/restoration exploration sim with minimal or no combat. The closest available archetypes are poor fits because they require identity-defining elements not supported here, such as farming/life loops, factory/engineering systems, colony/city management, survival pressures, shooter combat, or RPG progression. Because the gameplay identity is clear but falls outside the available archetype set, the title should be rejected rather than forced into an inaccurate category.

overall_note: The rail has a strong top end because several candidates match the anchor's cleanup-and-restoration core loop closely. The main gap is that most candidates are single-player and smaller in structure, so the anchor's co-op/community-play identity is underrepresented.
anchor_summary: Relaxing non-combat cleanup/restoration sim centered on vacuuming waste, recycling materials, rebuilding damaged areas, and seeing visible environmental recovery. Core identity is restorative tool use plus exploration, with solo/co-op and unusually large community multiplayer as a major differentiator.
gap_notes: No candidate really matches the anchor's large shared-zone/community multiplayer structure; most are solo experiences.
The set captures cleaning/restoration well, but fewer candidates reflect the anchor's exploration-of-hidden-secrets layer.
Several lower candidates drift toward renovation or farming/life-sim structures rather than pure environmental restoration.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Extremely close on relaxing cleanup, vacuum-and-water tool use, visible environmental recovery, light exploration, and efficiency upgrades; biggest difference is single-player-only scope.

Very strong fit on pollution cleanup, recycling, gadget upgrades, ecosystem revival, and exploratory restoration, though creature care and underwater adventure make it a bit more bespoke and less social.

Excellent match for satisfying low-pressure tool-driven cleaning and visible progress through spaces, but it is more job-based washing/business progression than environmental restoration and discovery.
Shares vacuuming trash, recycling, exploration, and restoring blighted environments, but farming, crafting, animal care, and village rebuilding shift the core loop toward life-sim.

Has the clean-fix-restore loop and visible transformation of damaged spaces, but station renovation/decorating is a looser renovation sim match rather than ecosystem cleanup or exploration-led restoration.

title: Darwin's Paradox!

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently frame Darwin's Paradox! as a single-player, story-driven 3D platformer-adventure with puzzle solving and light stealth. The strongest recurring gameplay signals are 'platforming sequences,' 'moments of puzzle-solving,' and verbs like swim, wall-climb, hide, infiltrate, jump, camouflage, and shoot ink to overcome traps and escape. The presentation is described as a cinematic animated-movie-like adventure in a mysterious industrial complex with sprawling handcrafted levels, which supports a linear/level-based campaign structure and a secondary cinematic action-adventure flavor. Combat is not presented as dominant; instead, stealthy avoidance, traversal, and puzzle progression appear to define the core loop, making action_platformer the best fit.

overall_note: This candidate set is tightly aligned with the anchor's likely gameplay identity: cinematic puzzle-platforming, vulnerable traversal through hostile spaces, and stealthy escape through authored levels. The main spread is between very close cinematic hazard-platformers and slightly looser companions with stronger companion or classic 2D systemic emphasis.
anchor_summary: Darwin's Paradox! reads as a single-player cinematic platform-adventure centered on authored progression, traversal, puzzle-solving, environmental hazards, and light stealth/evasion rather than combat-heavy action.
gap_notes: The rail leans heavily toward 2D/side-scrolling cinematic puzzle-platformers; if the anchor proves more fully 3D traversal-driven, some picks may feel slightly flatter in movement expression than the anchor.
No candidate strongly reflects the anchor's octopus-specific mobility toolkit like swimming, wall-climbing, and camouflage, so the set captures structure and feel more than exact moveset identity.
The strongest fit pattern here is low-combat escape/puzzle progression through dangerous industrial or oppressive spaces, which matches the available evidence well.

candidate_reviews.rationale: Extremely close on linear cinematic progression, environmental hazard navigation, puzzle-platforming, oppressive industrial mystery, and minimal-combat survival.
Very strong match for authored platform-adventure, stealthy evasion, chase sequences, oversized threats, and puzzle-led movement, though it leans harder into horror.
Strong escape-focused platform-adventure comp with stealth, traps, industrial oppression, and vulnerable progression, but its classic 2D cadence is more old-school and systemic.
Good fit on cinematic campaign structure, puzzle-solving, dangerous traversal, and light stealth, but the companion-driven design shifts the core loop somewhat away from the anchor.
Relevant for vulnerable linear puzzle-platforming and trap-heavy progression, but it is more stripped-down and less feature-complete as an adventure comparison than the stronger candidates.

title: Meow Moments: Celebrating Myth & Machine

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Multiple stored descriptions consistently identify the game as a cozy hidden-object adventure focused on finding cats, characters, and balloons across two illustrated scenes/settings. The copy emphasizes pressure-free, family-friendly search gameplay, detailed static environments, intuitive controls, and solo play, with no indication of combat, platforming, racing, or broader action-adventure systems. Metacritic also labels the genre as Hidden Object. This strongly supports hidden_object_puzzle as the core gameplay identity.

overall_note: Both candidates fit the anchor's core hidden-object cat-finding loop well. The set is directionally correct, though it is narrow and lacks a truly essential benchmark or especially close same-series equivalent.

title: Cooking Simulator 2: Better Together

proposed_taxonomy.evidence_summary: Stored store descriptions consistently identify a first-person cooking/restaurant simulation focused on preparing dishes, managing ingredients and stations, serving guests, experimenting with recipes, and optional co-op/sandbox play. However, the allowed taxonomy archetype list does not contain a clear fit for a first-person cooking or restaurant-work simulation. Available simulation-adjacent archetypes such as management_tycoon, life_sim, farming_sim, engineering_sandbox_sim, or transport_sim would misrepresent the game's primary hands-on culinary simulation loop.

overall_note: The set has one essential benchmark and several sensible culinary-sim neighbors, but most candidates drift toward broader restaurant management or adjacent food-production sims rather than matching the anchor's first-person tactile kitchen loop and co-op sandbox identity.
anchor_summary: First-person hands-on cooking/restaurant simulation centered on tactile food prep, kitchen workflow, recipe experimentation, guest service, progression/perks, and optional co-op/sandbox chaos. Best matches should preserve the direct chef-at-the-station simulation loop more than abstract restaurant management.
